title: Listening - Part 4
description: For each question, choose the correct answer.
instructions: You will hear an interview. For questions 20–25, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear.
context: You will hear an interview with a man called Ben Carter, who restores old boats.
audio_script: Interviewer: I'm joined by Ben Carter, who restores old wooden boats. Ben, where did your interest in boats begin? || Guest: My grandfather had a small boat, but I wasn't especially interested as a child. I preferred drawing and making things. After university I visited a harbour with a friend and saw an old boat being repaired. [20]The shapes of the wood and the smell of the workshop reminded me of art, and I wanted to try it myself[/20]. || Interviewer: Was it easy to learn? || Guest: Not at all. I joined a course and expected to be good because I was practical. But boats are complicated. [21]What surprised me most was how slowly you have to work, checking every piece before cutting it[/21]. If you hurry, you waste expensive wood. || Interviewer: What do you like most about restoring a boat? || Guest: The final day on the water is special, but I prefer an earlier stage. [22]I love finding out the boat's history from old photographs and from families who once owned it[/22]. It gives the work meaning. || Interviewer: Are customers easy to work with? || Guest: Usually, yes. They care about their boats, which is good. The only difficulty is cost. [23]People often think repairs will be quick, but hidden damage can make the price rise[/23], so I have to explain everything clearly. || Interviewer: You also teach young people sometimes. How do they react? || Guest: They often arrive thinking the work will be boring or old-fashioned. But after an hour they change. [24]They enjoy using real tools and seeing that careful handwork can still be useful[/24]. That's encouraging. || Interviewer: What are your plans now? || Guest: I could move to a bigger workshop, but I don't want too many projects at once. [25]I'm planning to restore one historic fishing boat and record the whole process online[/25], so more people can see how it's done. || Interviewer: Before we finish, Ben, do old boats ever make you feel connected to the people who built them? || Guest: Very much. Sometimes I find pencil marks under a seat or a repair that was done by hand fifty years ago. Those little signs tell you someone stood in the same place, solving the same kind of problem. I try not to remove that history unless it is unsafe. A perfect new surface is not always the right answer, because the age of the boat is part of its character. || Interviewer: What do people misunderstand about your work? || Guest: They think it is peaceful all the time. Some of it is, especially shaping wood quietly in the morning. But there is also dust, noise and heavy lifting. You need patience, but you also need strength and good planning. If the workshop is untidy, the work becomes dangerous very quickly. || Interviewer: Do you still enjoy going out on boats yourself? || Guest: Yes, although I now notice every small problem, which can annoy my friends. I look at the seats, the paint, the ropes and the corners. They just want to enjoy the trip, while I am thinking about repairs. I suppose that is what happens when your hobby becomes your job. || Interviewer: Finally, what do you do when you need new ideas? || Guest: I try to step away from the main work for a short time. Going for a walk, visiting another place or talking to someone outside the job usually helps. If I sit at a desk worrying, nothing improves. I also keep a small notebook, because useful ideas often arrive at inconvenient moments, like on a bus or while making dinner.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: I'm joined by Ben Carter, who restores old wooden boats. Ben, where did your interest in boats begin?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: My grandfather had a small boat, but I wasn't especially interested as a child. I preferred drawing and making things. After university I visited a harbour with a friend and saw an old boat being repaired. The shapes of the wood and the smell of the workshop reminded me of art, and I wanted to try it myself.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Was it easy to learn?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Not at all. I joined a course and expected to be good because I was practical. But boats are complicated. What surprised me most was how slowly you have to work, checking every piece before cutting it. If you hurry, you waste expensive wood.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What do you like most about restoring a boat?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: The final day on the water is special, but I prefer an earlier stage. I love finding out the boat's history from old photographs and from families who once owned it. It gives the work meaning.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Are customers easy to work with?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Usually, yes. They care about their boats, which is good. The only difficulty is cost. People often think repairs will be quick, but hidden damage can make the price rise, so I have to explain everything clearly.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: You also teach young people sometimes. How do they react?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: They often arrive thinking the work will be boring or old-fashioned. But after an hour they change. They enjoy using real tools and seeing that careful handwork can still be useful. That's encouraging.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What are your plans now?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I could move to a bigger workshop, but I don't want too many projects at once. I'm planning to restore one historic fishing boat and record the whole process online, so more people can see how it's done.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Before we finish, Ben, do old boats ever make you feel connected to the people who built them?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Very much. Sometimes I find pencil marks under a seat or a repair that was done by hand fifty years ago. Those little signs tell you someone stood in the same place, solving the same kind of problem. I try not to remove that history unless it is unsafe. A perfect new surface is not always the right answer, because the age of the boat is part of its character.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What do people misunderstand about your work?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: They think it is peaceful all the time. Some of it is, especially shaping wood quietly in the morning. But there is also dust, noise and heavy lifting. You need patience, but you also need strength and good planning. If the workshop is untidy, the work becomes dangerous very quickly.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do you still enjoy going out on boats yourself?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Yes, although I now notice every small problem, which can annoy my friends. I look at the seats, the paint, the ropes and the corners. They just want to enjoy the trip, while I am thinking about repairs. I suppose that is what happens when your hobby becomes your job.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Finally, what do you do when you need new ideas?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I try to step away from the main work for a short time. Going for a walk, visiting another place or talking to someone outside the job usually helps. If I sit at a desk worrying, nothing improves. I also keep a small notebook, because useful ideas often arrive at inconvenient moments, like on a bus or while making dinner.

questions.question: Why did Ben first want to restore boats?

options.A: He remembered using his grandfather's boat.
options.B: He wanted to work near the sea with a friend.
options.C: He was attracted by the artistic side of the work.

questions.explanation: Ben says the wood and workshop reminded him of art and made him want to try the work.

questions.question: What surprised Ben when he started learning?

options.A: how carefully each job had to be done
options.C: how many different boats there were

questions.explanation: He says he was surprised by how slowly he had to work and check each piece before cutting it.

questions.question: What does Ben most enjoy about restoring a boat?

options.A: taking the boat onto the water

questions.explanation: Ben says he loves finding out the boat's history from photographs and families.

questions.question: What problem does Ben often have with customers?

options.A: They change their minds about the design.
options.B: They do not understand why costs increase.
options.C: They want the work finished too carefully.

questions.explanation: He says hidden damage can make the price rise, so he has to explain this clearly.

questions.question: What do young people enjoy in Ben's classes?
questions.explanation: Ben says young people enjoy using real tools and seeing handwork is useful.

questions.question: What does Ben plan to do next?

options.C: repair several boats at the same time

questions.explanation: He says he plans to restore a historic fishing boat and record the process online.
